info.text: In this demo I’ve imported the D3 library created by the data visualization company Observable, a powerful toolkit for working with data and generating SVG programatically popular in the data-viz community. D3 gives you building blocks like selections, data joins, and scales, so you can generate exactly the sort of chart or graph you want.
info.title: getting started
settings
info.text: We start inside a setup function by defining a tiny dataset and a few layout variables: width, height, and barWidth. This is just us deciding how big the drawing should be and how wide each bar ought to be (total width divided by number of data points).

info.title: svg
info.text: Next we create the drawing context: an svg element. With D3, we select an element that already exists (in this case body), append a new element to it (our svg), and set attributes on it (width and height). SVG is like a tiny canvas where each shape (like a rectangle) is a real DOM node you can style, animate and interact with.

info.title: data join
info.text: Here’s where D3 joins our data with our SVG (the visuals), svg.selectAll('rect') asks D3 for all the rectangles in the SVG (none yet). .data(vals) binds our array of numbers to that selection. .enter() represents the incoming data points that need elements, and .append('rect') creates one rectangle per value. That’s how D3 turns data into SVG elements.

info.title: bar attributes
info.text: Each bar’s attributes are set from the data. x uses the index to place bars left-to-right: i * barWidth. width is barWidth - 5 so we get a small gap between bars. height literally comes from the data value originally defined in vals.

info.title: coordinate system
info.text: SVG’s coordinate system starts at the top-left, with y increasing downward. That’s why we set y to height - d: it moves the top of the bar up by its value so bars get drawn upward from the bottom edge (otherwise the graph would look upside down).

info.title: fill color
info.text: This last method is used to change the SVG element's CSS, we're using it here to give the bars a fill color of teal.

info.title: on load
info.text: As with many of our other demos, the last line in our code sets everything going by running our setup function as soon as our page has loaded.

info.title: lots more
info.text: There’s so much more you can do with this library, this is only the beginning. D3 is an incredibly deep toolkit, beyond simple bar charts it can be used to build interactive visualizations, scatter plots, maps, force-directed graphs, and streamgraphs that respond to user interaction or live data. It also plays well with other libraries and frameworks.
